data.title: Takeda plans to lay off 400 employees in US - report
data.description:
• Takeda Pharmaceutical (TAK) plans to lay off 400 employees in the US to boost profitability.
  • The Japanese pharma aims to make the change ahead of a planned CEO in June, Nikkei reported.
  • The Japanese newspaper noted that Takeda plans investments in "blood products and other priority areas."

data.title: U.S. stocks inch up as Wall Street assesses the latest labor report; oil prices ease
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages crawled higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) leading the advance as oil prices fell for the first time since the U.S.-Israel-Iran war escalations and chip stocks recovered. 

Traders also digested a better-than-expected labor report and a lower U.S. Composite PMI read.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.9%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.5%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.7%.

U.S. private sector employment grew by 63K jobs in February, topping the +43K consensus and accelerating from the 11K added in January, according to data released by ADP on Wednesday.

In addition, the February S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI came in lower than expected at 51.9 from 53.0 in January. Lastly, mortgage refinance applications increased for the fourth straight week to the strongest pace since 2022.

“My main pushback on the AI doomsaying for the labor market is that AI is not in charge; we are. Leadership matters. Also, policy (at various levels) can help encourage pro-worker AI outcomes other than automation. The private market on its own might not do enough,” said Claudia Sahm, chief economist for New Century Advisors.

On commodities, oil prices (CL1:COM) fell on Wednesday for the first time since the start of the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

U.S. Treasury yields were slightly higher across the curve.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was mostly flat at 4.07%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) advanced 2 basis points to 3.53%.

The Trump Administration is expected to implement the 15% global tariff rate sometime this week, according to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

data.title: Markets can weather fewer interest rates due to corporate profits, solid fundamentals – Deutsche Bank’s Chadha
data.description:
The stock market can weather a scenario with fewer or even no interest rate cuts this year, according to Bankim Chadha, chief U.S. equity and global strategist at Deutsche Bank.

In an interview with CNBC, Chadha noted that market pricing has already adjusted to reflect expectations of minimal cuts, closely aligning with Deutsche Bank’s forecast of just one more cut this year.

The strategist emphasized that the market’s stability hinges primarily on the strength of corporate earnings rather than Federal Reserve policy moves.

Whether the Fed delivers one cut or two, Chadha argued it won’t significantly alter the market’s trajectory.

“The real issue is just really earnings and whether they hold up, and we think they do,” he said. With little rate relief currently priced into markets, investors have shifted their focus to fundamentals rather than monetary policy expectations.

Chadha pointed to robust earnings growth as a key support for equities, noting that corporate profits are expanding at an annual rate of approximately 15%. Because stock prices have remained range-bound while earnings have climbed, he observed that valuations have actually become more attractive.

“Stocks are a little bit cheaper after three months,” he said, expressing a constructive outlook for markets three months out and beyond.

The S&P 500 (SP500) has maintained a trading range established back in December, even as investors have broadly reduced positioning across asset classes, including gold (GLD), (IAU), (SGOL), (OUNZ).

This move toward neutral has triggered what Chadha described as a “swapping of the winners and the losers,” with software stocks (IGPT), (XSW), (IGV) and other previously out-of-favor sectors seeing a bounce.

Economic indicators are providing additional support, with ISM manufacturing and services data looking “pretty good,” according to Chadha.

He noted that an inflation scare appears to be receding based on the latest readings. “It would be the biggest irony if these events led us to break the three-month range to the other side, but that’s because stocks are a little cheaper and growth is a little bit better,” he observed.

The strategist also addressed the divergence between U.S. and international markets, particularly the sharp selloff in Asian equities (EEMA), (ASEA), (AIA). South Korea’s (EWY), (FLKR), (KORU) memory-chip-heavy market faced a “double whammy” as investors unwound positions while the U.S. dollar rallied, Chadha explained.

Markets had been positioned long on international equities relative to the U.S., and the recent risk reduction has reversed those bets.

data.title: Boliden halts Kevitsa mine expansion plan, citing Finland mining tax hike
data.description:
Swedish miner Boliden (BDNNY) said Wednesday it is halting plans to spend ~€1B ($1.16B) to expand its Kevitsa copper and nickel mine in Finland, affecting up to 285 employees as it prepares to adapt its operations at the site after a new mining tax was implemented in the country.

"Due to the quadrupling of mining tax in Finland, Boliden Kevitsa has called for change negotiations to prepare to adopt the operations," the miner said.

Finland introduced new mining taxes this year that have raised the tax on metal ores such as copper, nickel, and zinc to 2.5% of the taxable value of the metal contained in mined material, from 0.6% previously.

"We believe mining of metals such as nickel and copper should be safeguarded in Europe through competitive long-term business conditions. Sadly, it appears that Finland has chosen a path that creates challenges for the capital-intensive mining industry," Boliden Kevitsa (BDNNY) general manager Tom Soderman said.

data.title: MEC outlines $3.2B data center market opportunity and targets more than 20% revenue from segment in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mayville Engineering Company, Inc. (MEC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jagadeesh Reddy, President, CEO & Director, described the fourth quarter as a transitional period, stating that “our team remained focused on positioning the business for successful execution and growth as we enter 2026.” Reddy highlighted robust demand momentum in the Data Center & Critical Power end market, with proactive reallocation of resources to support project launches. He emphasized, “our fourth quarter margin performance was pressured...reflecting deliberate investments to support program readiness and execution.” Reddy asserted these margin pressures are “transitory in nature and importantly, position MEC to deliver profitable growth in 2026 and beyond.” The company’s qualified opportunity pipeline in Data Center & Critical Power now exceeds $125 million, with projects scheduled to launch in 2026 valued at approximately $40 million to $50 million, and Reddy noted, “we expect Data Center & Critical Power to represent more than 20% of our revenues in 2026.”

  • Reddy announced new project awards totaling approximately $15 million with Data Center & Critical Power customers in the fourth quarter, and year-to-date total awards across legacy markets surpassed $108 million, exceeding the annual target. For 2026, the company expects total bookings across end markets to be about $140 million.

  • On capital allocation, Reddy stated, “our primary use of free cash flow will remain focused on debt reduction,” with a long-term target of 2.5x leverage. He highlighted disciplined capital deployment and the introduction of quarterly financial guidance.

  • Rachele Lehr, Chief Financial Officer, reported, “Total sales for the fourth quarter increased 10.7% on a year-over-year basis to $134.3 million.” Lehr noted the manufacturing margin rate was 6.6% for the fourth quarter, attributing the decrease to project launch costs and early-stage inefficiencies. She stated, “Adjusted EBITDA margin was 4.7% for the quarter compared to 7.6% in the prior year period...excluding these items, adjusted EBITDA margin would have been approximately 7%.” Lehr also reported free cash flow of $10.2 million and end-of-quarter net debt of $205.3 million.

Outlook

  • Lehr introduced quarterly guidance for the first time, projecting first quarter 2026 net sales between $137 million and $143 million, and adjusted EBITDA between $5 million and $7 million. For the full year 2026, MEC expects net sales of $580 million to $620 million, adjusted EBITDA of $50 million to $60 million, and free cash flow of $25 million to $35 million. The outlook includes $40 million to $50 million in incremental cross-selling revenue and assumes improvement in legacy end markets in the second half of the year. Lehr added, “we expect our free cash flow conversion for the full year to be between approximately 50% and 60% of adjusted EBITDA.”

Financial Results

  • The company reported total sales of $134.3 million for Q4, up 10.7% year-over-year. Organic net sales declined by 5.3%. Manufacturing margin rate decreased to 6.6%, impacted by $1.2 million in project launch costs and $1.7 million in early-stage inefficiencies. Free cash flow was $10.2 million, and net debt rose to $205.3 million with a leverage ratio of 3.7x. Adjusted EBITDA margin was 4.7%.

Q&A

  • Linda Umwali, D.A. Davidson & Co.: Asked about the impact of strong Class 8 truck orders on 2026 outlook and potential order pull-forward. Reddy responded, “We have been signals from our OEMs...of potential build rate increases...we expect some of this demand to accelerate the build rate increases from our CV customers, and we expect that to start showing up in mid- to late Q2.”

  • Umwali inquired about agricultural market recovery. Reddy explained, “large ag will still be down this year, double digits...but we are seeing signs of improvement in small ag, lawn care, turf and forestry equipment.”

  • Umwali asked about Critical Power launch costs. Lehr answered, “we really expect and anticipate most of that to start taking place to be at full run rate at the end of the second quarter...we expect to incur more of those costs having the margin pressure in the first half.”

  • Greg Palm, Craig-Hallum: Queried about lower-than-expected margins and EBITDA guidance. Reddy attributed this to “more business in data centers than we anticipated coming out of our November call.” Lehr added that expedited launches required significant resource investments.

  • Palm asked about pipeline visibility. Reddy replied, “we have good line of sight to that $120 million worth of data center business for the year...most of that is visibility and greater than 50% confidence.”

  • Robert Samuel Karlov, William Blair: Asked about EBITDA guidance components. Lehr explained the continued muted volumes in legacy business, high fixed costs, and ongoing talent retention, with launch costs expected to taper down through the year.

  • Natalia Bak, Citi: Questioned customer concentration in Data Center & Critical Power. Reddy responded, “Within that end market is reasonably diversified...we also are working with the next tier of OEMs.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on clarification of margin pressures, pipeline visibility, and capacity for growth, with a slightly positive but cautious tone, evidenced by repeated questions on market recovery timing and guidance breakdown.

  • Management maintained a confident and proactive stance in prepared remarks, emphasizing future growth, but displayed some defensiveness and caution in Q&A, especially regarding transitory margin pressures and capacity planning. Reddy stated, “we expect...margin pressure is primarily driven by early stage project inefficiencies and project launch costs as we prepare for higher volume programs.”

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased focus on the speed and scale of data center opportunities, with a more forward-looking tone and specific questions about execution risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter’s call placed greater emphasis on the rapid expansion of the Data Center & Critical Power segment and the introduction of quarterly guidance. The pipeline has grown, with revenue synergy expectations from Accu-Fab cross-selling rising from $20-$30 million (Q3) to $40-$50 million (Q4) for 2026. Management maintained a focus on disciplined capital allocation and operational improvements.

  • Analyst questions shifted from concerns about market softness and integration in Q3 to execution of aggressive growth, margin normalization, and resource allocation in Q4. Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to proactive confidence in capturing new opportunities, while acknowledging near-term cost pressures as necessary investments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Margin pressure from early-stage project inefficiencies and launch costs was highlighted as a transitory but significant challenge.

  • Ongoing muted volumes in legacy markets and high fixed costs were cited as ongoing risks.

  • Increasing debt and leverage stemming from the Accu-Fab acquisition were noted, with management’s mitigation focused on debt reduction and operational excellence initiatives.

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the pace of recovery in commercial vehicle and agricultural markets, as well as the ability to manage capacity for simultaneous growth in legacy and new markets.

Final Takeaway

MEC’s management highlighted that 2026 will be a pivotal year, driven by significant expansion in Data Center & Critical Power, with cross-selling synergies projected at $40 million to $50 million and the segment expected to represent over 20% of revenues. The company is proactively investing in project launches and operational improvements, with a clear focus on capacity management and debt reduction, aiming to position MEC for profitable growth as legacy markets recover and data center opportunities accelerate.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: How much fiscal firepower does the U.S. have?
data.description:
The expanding conflict with Iran comes as the U.S. government is already running large peacetime deficits.

Overall, the Congressional Budget Office is projecting a federal deficit of about $1.9T in fiscal-year 2026, with government debt continuing to swell to $3.1T by 2036. The shortfall is already running at an unusually large 6% of gross domestic product.

Defense spending is already substantial in the U.S.—one of the largest government outlays. In February, Congress passed an FY26 appropriations bill providing some $839B in funding for the Pentagon, representing roughly 3% of GDP, well below Cold War levels of ~6%.

But with current huge federal deficits, the capacity to finance major military options rests largely on the government's ability to issue more Treasury debt (US10Y) (US2Y) (US30Y) and the market's willingness to absorb it. That puts investor focus on demand at government bond auctions, which recently has been solid.

Kent Smetters, director of the Penn Wharton Budget Model, has reportedly said that the U.S.-Israel military campaign against Iran could cost up to about $210B, depending on how long the conflict lasts, which could be weeks or "whatever it takes."

Meanwhile, the Senate is set to vote Wednesday on a war powers resolution that would require congressional approval for further military action against Iran, following President Donald Trump’s unauthorized decision to launch military action against Iran.

Treasury exchange-traded funds: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), and (BIL).

data.title: Orion Group targets $900M–$950M revenue for 2026 as pipeline grows to $23B with McAmis acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Orion Group Holdings (ORN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Travis Boone highlighted 2025 as a year marked by "strong operational execution and meaningful advancement of Orion's long-term strategic priorities." Boone emphasized both top and bottom line growth, robust free cash flow, and a strengthened balance sheet, stating, "We drove both top and bottom line growth and generated good free cash flow. Across the organization, our team delivered with predictable excellence, executing projects safely and profitably, strengthening our balance sheet and taking important strategic steps that position our company for continued growth ahead."
  • Boone announced the closing of a new $120 million senior credit facility in December, noting it "improves our liquidity, lowers our cost of capital and provides flexibility to support both organic growth and accretive acquisitions." He also referenced the acquisition of a large Jones Act derrick barge to expand marine and defense-related project capacity, and the completed acquisition of J.E. McAmis, which "greatly enhances our marine platform, particularly in complex jetty and breakwater construction."
  • The company consolidated its Houston footprint, modernized project management systems, settled litigation, and monetized nonstrategic real estate, all of which Boone positioned as strategic moves for future growth.
  • Boone acknowledged backlog as an area that "was not as we anticipated," with $763 million in new contracts and a 0.9x book-to-bill, attributing it to "tariff-related uncertainty in the private sector at the beginning of the year, followed by the prolonged U.S. government shutdown later in the year."
  • Boone stated confidence in the demand outlook, referencing a "vibrant growing pipeline that is currently at $23 billion, which includes the J.E. McAmis pipeline of $1.4 billion."
  • CFO Alison Vasquez reported, "For the full year 2025, revenue increased to $852 million, operating income to $15 million, adjusted EBITDA to $45 million and adjusted EPS to $0.25 per share... full year operating cash flow of $28 million and free cash flow of $14 million."
  • Vasquez detailed Marine segment performance: "Marine delivered $545 million of revenue, a 4.5% annual growth and more than doubled its adjusted EBITDA to $56 million for the year. This represents a 10% adjusted EBITDA margin compared to about 5% in 2024."
  • Concrete segment revenues increased 12% to $307 million, though Concrete reported an $11 million loss in adjusted EBITDA, attributed to corporate allocations and the absence of prior year project closeout benefits.
  • Vasquez disclosed a plan to "break out corporate expenses separately as a nonoperating segment and will no longer allocate those costs to Marine and Concrete for external reporting purposes," starting Q1 2026.
  • Vasquez highlighted the new $120 million UMB Bank credit agreement, which "reduces borrowing costs, extends maturity by 2 years and positions the balance sheet to fund future investments." Borrowings under the UMB credit facility bear interest at SOFR plus 2.5% to 3%, described as a "40% reduction in our borrowing costs compared to the prior credit agreement."

Outlook

  • Vasquez provided 2026 guidance: "revenue in the range of $900 million to $950 million, a 9% increase from 2025 at the midpoint; adjusted EBITDA in the range of $54 million to $58 million, a 24% increase from 2025 at the midpoint; adjusted EPS in the range of $0.36 to $0.42, a 56% increase from 2025 at the midpoint; and capital expenditures in the range of $25 million to $35 million, consistent with last year."
  • Management expects "modest margin expansion across the business, both from the favorable impacts of blending McAmis into the Marine business," with McAmis operating at higher margins, and "concrete will deliver margins in the mid-single digits."

Financial Results

  • Reported revenue for 2025 reached $852 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $45 million and adjusted EPS of $0.25 per share. Marine revenue was $545 million, and Concrete revenue totaled $307 million. Operating cash flow for the year was $28 million, and free cash flow was $14 million.
  • Vasquez noted net debt at year-end was approximately $6 million, following the refinancing and property sale, and subsequent borrowing of $47 million in February to fund the McAmis acquisition.

Q&A

  • Tomohiko Sano, JPMorgan: Asked about Q4 revenue recognition delays and margin impacts. Vasquez responded, "the fourth quarter came in generally in line with what we expected. We didn't see a lot of softness in the quarter... things came in, in line, including from a corporate perspective."
  • Sano followed up on 2026 margin outlook. Vasquez: "We are continuing to expect that we will have modest margin expansion across the business, both from the favorable impacts of blending McAmis into the Marine business...And then from a concrete perspective, we do expect that concrete will deliver margins in the mid-single digits."
  • Sano inquired about data center impact. Boone indicated "right now, it's 40% of our concrete business is data centers. I expect that to probably go up a little in the next year."
  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: Questioned pipeline timing and margin trends. Boone described multiple near-term $100 million-plus opportunities and stated, "We're one job away, one project win away from the backlog being in good shape."
  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH Capital: Asked about marine project timing and pipeline health. Vasquez said, "We continue to have our good line of sight into $8 billion, $8.5 billion of opportunities that we expect to be awarded in 2026...that number continues to sit at right around $1 billion."
  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital: Queried historical win rates and EBITDA guidance bridge. Boone replied, "win rate... ticked up...we have seen it head in the right direction, just a little bit, 1% or 2%."
  • Rygiel also asked about backlog outlook. Vasquez: "we do expect that to meaningfully deliver good bookings, which ultimately will serve to elevate our backlog balances."
  • Liam Burke, B. Riley: Sought clarity on derrick barge return and M&A pipeline. Boone said, "we've got some work being done on it for the next, I don't know, 6 -- let's say, 6 to 8 months...we'll get it busy and get it operational."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts consistently pressed for detail on backlog, margin expectations, and data center growth, reflecting a slightly positive to neutral tone, open to management's optimism but focused on execution risks and timing.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone, especially in prepared remarks, though responses during Q&A occasionally shifted to clarifications and guarded optimism, with Boone stating, "We're not worried. We're bidding projects in the real near term here that we feel good about."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained generally constructive dialogue, with management showing more confidence due to recent acquisitions and improved liquidity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • 2026 guidance was raised to higher ranges for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and EPS versus the prior quarter, reflecting increased confidence after closing the McAmis acquisition and strengthening the balance sheet.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward M&A integration, expanded data center work, and enhanced project management systems, compared to previous emphasis on bonding capacity and backlog build.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on pipeline visibility, data center contributions, and backlog conversion, though Q4 saw deeper questions on margin expansion and segment-specific growth drivers.
  • Management's tone in Q4 was more assertive about future growth, referencing a $23 billion pipeline and continued margin expansion, while the previous quarter focused on foundational progress and external headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Boone cited backlog as an area below expectations due to "customer decisions moved to the right primarily due to tariff-related uncertainty in the private sector at the beginning of the year, followed by the prolonged U.S. government shutdown later in the year."
  • Management acknowledged the "fluid" geopolitical situation in the Middle East and its potential to impact business and markets, stating they are "actively monitoring developments and evaluating any potential impacts."
  • Analysts questioned timing of project awards and backlog conversion, with Vasquez highlighting a persistent $1 billion in projects "awaiting award from the client."

Final Takeaway

Orion Group Holdings management underscored a year of operational progress, improved liquidity, and strategic expansion with the McAmis acquisition and a new credit facility. With a $23 billion pipeline, increased focus on higher-margin marine and expanding data center projects, and guidance projecting up to $950 million in revenue for 2026, the company positions itself for continued growth amid some near-term backlog timing risks and dynamic market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Netflix adds more capabilities to its advertising platform
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) announced on Wednesday that it is expanding its Ads Suite to give brands more options to buy and measure ads, with a focus on better targeting, frequency management, and scaled reach on its ad-supported plan. The streaming giant is introducing new retail and behavioral audience capabilities starting in the second quarter in the U.S., with other ad-supported markets to follow. 

Through Amazon (AMZN) DSP, advertisers can use Amazon Audiences, which is built from extensive shopping, streaming, and browsing signals, to reach Netflix (NFLX) members based on real behaviors, lifestyles, interests, and active shopping intent. Through Yahoo DSP, advertisers can activate deterministic Yahoo audiences informed by hundreds of millions of global interest, behavioral, purchase, and life-stage signals, which is anticipated to make campaigns feel more timely and relevant for viewers.

Netflix (NFLX) is also launching its own conversion API to address advertiser demand for full-funnel solutions and clearer proof of outcomes. The CAPI is designed to use real-time insights to optimize campaigns and demonstrate results. In early testing with Tinuiti, a large independent full-funnel agency in the U.S., campaigns using Netflix's (NFLX) tools beat benchmarks by more than 75% across financial services, ed tech, and retail clients. 

"These advancements are part of our ongoing effort to make advertising on Netflix more effective and easier to buy. We’re offering our brand partners clearer ways to reach the right audiences while maintaining the high-quality experience our members expect," highlighted the company.

The development could be relevant for the Trade Desk (TTD), which also operates a demand-side platform that helps advertisers buy and optimize data-driven digital ad campaigns across the open internet in one place.

Netflix (NFLX) was up 1.0% in midday action, while The Trade Desk (TTD) traded flat.

data.title: Dingdong Non-GAAP EPS of $0.02, revenue of $892.7M
data.description:
• Dingdong press release (DDL): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.02.
  • Revenue of $892.7M (+10.3% Y/Y).
  • Product revenues were RMB6,164.6 million (US$881.5 million) compared with product revenues of RMB5,822.5 million in the same quarter of 2024, an increase of 5.9% year over year.
  • Service revenues were RMB78.0 million (US$11.2 million) compared with service revenues of RMB82.5 million in the same quarter of 2024, a decrease of 5.4% year over year.
